title: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR CODEX CONTACT POINTS AND NATIONAL CODEX COMMITTEES (AFRICA) CAC/GL 43R-2003

sections.section: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR CODEX CONTACT POINTS AND NATIONAL CODEX COMMITTEES (AFRICA) CAC/GL 43R-2003
sections.summary: Document title only: regional guidelines for Codex Contact Points and National Codex Committees (Africa), CAC/GL 43R‑2003.

Introduces the Codex Alimentarius Commission as the FAO/WHO food standards programme, explains its global role in protecting public health and facilitating fair food trade, and states the document's purpose to provide guidelines for establishing and improving Codex Contact Points and National Codex Committees to enhance national participation.

Recommends every Codex member establish a Codex Contact Point to serve as the official national link and communication agent with the Commission, facilitating active participation while not supplanting government roles, and functioning as a national codex secretariat headed by a competent person who also serves as NCC secretary.

Defines the Codex Contact Point as the official link between the member country and the Codex Secretariat, responsible for national coordination of Codex matters and headed by a knowledgeable person who acts as secretary to the National Codex Committee.

Lists primary functions of the Codex Contact Point including receiving and distributing Codex documents, studying and initiating action (directly or via the NCC), maintaining a Codex library, serving as liaison with the Codex Secretariat and other members, creating/promoting a website, and performing tasks recommended by the NCC or government.

States that the member country decides where to locate the Codex Contact Point—commonly hosted by government (e.g., Ministry of Health/Agriculture, National Bureau of Standards) for neutrality and coordination—and that the site should be neutral, capable of performing Contact Point functions, and accessible to stakeholders.

Sets out basic, non‑mandatory requirements for a Codex Contact Point such as committed staff, adequate office space, communication facilities (telephone, fax, email), stationery, and printing/photocopying equipment (computers, printers, photocopiers).

Explains the need to establish a National Codex Committee (NCC) as a consultative forum including all relevant stakeholders to formulate national positions on Codex matters, represent groups unable to attend international meetings, and advise government.

Outlines primary NCC functions: formulate the country's responses to Codex proposals, nominate delegates to Codex meetings, advise government on standards and implementation, appoint technical sub‑committees, and undertake other government‑advised duties.

Describes NCC composition and structure—ideally including relevant government ministries, consumers' organisations, industry, traders, universities, research institutions and experts—notes the chairperson (elected) and that the Codex Contact Point head serves as NCC secretary with duties (minutes, records, agendas), and sets procedural expectations (quorum, circulation, consensus decision‑making and reporting by subcommittees).

States that sustainable, transparent funding is needed for the Codex Contact Point and NCC, contributions must not be conditional, and possible sources include central government, foundations/trusts, industry levies, UN organisations (WHO, FAO, UNDP) and international organisations.

Annex enumerates the core functions of national Codex Contact Points, requiring them to act as the liaison between the Codex Secretariat and their country, coordinate national Codex activities, receive and circulate Codex final texts and working documents, submit comments to Codex bodies, cooperate with the National Codex Committee and liaise with industry, consumers and traders, exchange information domestically and with other members, handle participant notifications for Codex sessions, maintain a library of Codex texts, and promote Codex activities nationally.

sections.section: 5. CORE FUNCTIONS OF CODEX CONTACT POINTS
sections.summary: Codex Contact Points, whose operation varies by national legislation, must serve as the national link to the Codex Secretariat by coordinating all national Codex activities; receiving and circulating Codex final texts and working documents; submitting comments to the Commission, subsidiary bodies or Secretariat; liaising with National Codex Committees and stakeholders to provide balanced policy and technical advice; facilitating information exchange and coordination with other members; receiving session invitations and reporting participant names; maintaining a library of Codex final texts; and promoting Codex activities domestically.
